content: Hello, I want to experiment with adding a crate as a dependency for an out-of-tree kernel module (serde-cbor specifically). I've already looked through Using 3rd party crates in an out-of-tree module and Adding crates, also I've used cargo-vendor to vendor all the dependencies into a single folder. However, I can't seem to figure out how to write the Kbuild such that it builds each crate in the hierarchy and then links the dependencies to my kernel module. For example, for a given crate, how can I pass the necessary --external rustc flags and then tell it to build a library with the crate's name, starting from lib.rs? I've stumbled upon KBUILD_RUSTFLAGS_MODULE but I wonder if there's a way which doesn't involve reinventing the cargo build system.

The only thing I can come up with is to use an utility to read the Cargo.lock file which would then spit out Makefile targets and flags variables into one specific file. Later you could include it into your root Makefile and use some of the exposed flags and targets to build whatever depended on it.
Thanks, I guess it's just not a common enough use case, otherwise I would imagine there would have been an easier way to do it

Please be aware that if you add third-party crates, your code is likely going to be hard to upstream. (Previous discussion at https://rust-for-linux.zulipchat.com/#narrow/stream/384413-Filesystems/topic/FS/near/348298262).

However, I can't seem to figure out how to write the Kbuild such that it builds each crate in the hierarchy and then links the dependencies to my kernel module.

Since you are vendoring the code, for some libraries, you might be able to just "inline" the code as part of your kernel module crate. Otherwise, you will need to set up make to build the graph of crates.
I will think about ditching the dependencies, but serde would have made my life easier
What are you using it for, in particular? (collecting use cases is a good idea to justify adding it to the kernel in the future)

a container filesystem: puzzlefs, currently it's implemented in userspace with FUSE, but the plan is to also have an in-kernel implementation; in the optimal scenario I could reuse some of the existing code in my kernel driver, but it seems unlikely at the moment
filesystem metadata is cbor encoded, so that's why I need serde_cbor, but I imagine I would need serde even if I switch the encoding to something else
I see, thanks! Would you need serde for a particular reason? Would other serialization libraries work for you (or not, e.g. you want to share code with your userspace implementation)?

one reason would be that it's the de-facto standard, most of the serialization libraries use serde; sharing code with the userspace implementation would be also nice, so that would be another reason; but if there would be another serialization library in the kernel that I could use, I wouldn't mind
we even use custom encodings using serde, so it's nice that it's not tied to a particular format; I think it's a good candidate for kernel inclusion, since it's a generic framework

Miguel Ojeda said:

Please be aware that if you add third-party crates, your code is likely going to be hard to upstream. (Previous discussion at https://rust-for-linux.zulipchat.com/#narrow/stream/384413-Filesystems/topic/FS/near/348298262).

I think it is important to recognize that not all code are intended to be included in mainline. Some code might be impossible to upstream because of licence issues, or perhaps the code is not intended to be open sourced ever. While others do not agree, I support the idea of having a build system for out-of-tree modules that include cargo support. I think this is a means of enabling even more kernel code to be written in Rust, even if this code will never make it into mainline Linux.

The problem with this approach is that some projects might might start relying on the features provided by this out-of-tree build system (cargo and huge dependency graphs) just for convenience, even though they could maybe use other means and might have been candidates for upstream. Such a build system should come with HUGE warning banners about the consequences of not following upstream guidelines.

Either way, someone is going to build this system eventually, and we might as well support it to get said warnings into the system.

I think it is important to recognize that not all code are intended to be included in mainline.

Nobody has said otherwise! :) In fact, that is fine and supported.

The question here is not to support out-of-tree code or not, but whether to support a feature which may never land upstream and may never be available for in-tree modules.

Ideally such a feature (i.e. easily reusing existing crates) would land for both sides at the same time (even if, for in-tree, individual third-party crates would need to be explicitly allowed one-by-one), potentially as part of the new build system.

Some code might be impossible to upstream because of licence issues, or perhaps the code is not intended to be open sourced ever.

Side-note: to be clear, if there are licenses issues, then they really need to be careful about what they are doing, even if they are out-of-tree, because distributing those kernel modules may not be legal.

On top of that, core Rust symbols are exported with EXPORT_SYMBOL_GPL, and thus the kernel module loader actively refuses to link proprietary modules against them (i.e. bypassing that may be easily seen as a deliberate action).

The problem with this approach is that some projects might might start relying on the features provided by this out-of-tree build system (cargo and huge dependency graphs) just for convenience, even though they could maybe use other means and might have been candidates for upstream.

This is definitely an issue, but as long as the feature is supported in-tree and it is just a matter of allowing/disallowing crates in a list and warning the user otherwise, it should be fine.

Either way, someone is going to build this system eventually, and we might as well support it to get said warnings into the system.

If upstream does not want a feature, then, of course, it would not be a surprise if somebody supports it outside anyway; but we are focused on getting things upstreamed, i.e. available for everybody.

Hey @Miguel Ojeda , you've mentioned there were some discussions about potentially supporting third-party crates in the future, is this topic tracked somewhere (like a github issue or discussion)? I'm interested in following this topic, thanks.
At some point we will have the discussion in the mailing list most likely, so please subscribe to it.
Later on we can also create a tracking issue with e.g. a list of crates that are under discussion or similar.

I'm trying to run the following command which I see in the output of make V=1 LLVM=1:

  RUST_MODFILE=samples/rust/rust_fs rustc --edition=2021 -Zbinary_dep_depinfo=y -Dunsafe_op_in_unsafe_fn -Drust_2018_idioms -Dunreachable_pub -Dnon_ascii_idents -Wmissing_docs -Drustdoc::missing_crate_level_docs -Dclippy::correctness -Dclippy::style -Dclippy::suspicious -Dclippy::complexity -Dclippy::perf -Dclippy::let_unit_value -Dclippy::mut_mut -Dclippy::needless_bitwise_bool -Dclippy::needless_continue -Wclippy::dbg_macro --target=./scripts/target.json -Cpanic=abort -Cembed-bitcode=n -Clto=n -Cforce-unwind-tables=n -Ccodegen-units=1 -Csymbol-mangling-version=v0 -Crelocation-model=static -Zfunction-sections=n -Dclippy::float_arithmetic -Ctarget-feature=-sse,-sse2,-sse3,-ssse3,-sse4.1,-sse4.2,-avx,-avx2 -Ztune-cpu=generic -Cno-redzone=y -Ccode-model=kernel -Copt-level=2 -Cdebug-assertions=n -Coverflow-checks=y -Cforce-frame-pointers=y -Cdebuginfo=2  --cfg MODULE  @./include/generated/rustc_cfg -Zallow-features=allocator_api,core_ffi_c,explicit_generic_args_with_impl_trait,new_uninit,pin_macro -Zcrate-attr=no_std -Zcrate-attr='feature(allocator_api,core_ffi_c,explicit_generic_args_with_impl_trait,new_uninit,pin_macro)' --extern alloc --extern kernel --crate-type rlib -L ./rust/ --crate-name rust_fs --emit=dep-info=samples/rust/.rust_fs.o.d --emit=obj=samples/rust/rust_fs.o samples/rust/rust_fs.rs

but I get the following error:

error: the option `Z` is only accepted on the nightly compiler

so I'm wondering what's different in the kernel environment so that it runs this command successfully instead of erroring out
RUSTC_BOOTSTRAP=1
Having said that, why do you need to run the command manually, if I may ask?
(in case there is something we could provide)
Also note that we have single-target support, e.g. make LLVM=1 samples/rust/rust_minimal.o

Well I'm still trying to add serde to my kernel module, I've tried the inline method where I reorganize all the files and include one into another with mod, but that only works up to a point, e.g. serde_derive requires --crate-type=proc_macro, so I cannot compile my kernel module using a single compilation unit.
So what I'm exploring now is configuring cargo to build my dependencies within the kernel environment, and then linking them using KBuild when I build my kernel module, something like:

    $(MAKE) -C $(KDIR) KBUILD_RUSTFLAGS_MODULE="--extern serde=/home/amiculas/work/hello-rust/puzzlefs-kernel-module/target/debug/deps/libserde-d953150f01381e44.rmeta --extern serde_cbor=/home/amiculas/work/hello-rust/puzzlefs-kernel-module/target/debug/deps/libserde_cbor-b1bea3aaee98c338.rmeta" M=$$PWD

I don't know how good of an idea this is, but I think I need at least the ability to compile each crate individually.
For proc macros, you may put them inside rust/macros/.
I can give you a (not-for-upstreaming-yet) branch with serde integrated, if that helps. We have a similar one for syn experiments.
yeah, that would help a lot :)
can you please share these branches with me, @Miguel Ojeda ?

I meant that I could prepare a branch with serde.

The syn one is here, but it is fairly old, and based on rust: https://github.com/Rust-for-Linux/linux/pull/910

So I rebased that one on top of Linus', then added serde and serde_derive on top, and a sample that uses a data format defined in kernel which then gets used in the sample kernel module for a custom type via derive. So this works:

#[derive(Serialize, Deserialize, Debug)]
struct S {
    a: (),
    b: bool,
    c: bool,
    d: (),
}

...

let original = S {
    a: (),
    b: false,
    c: true,
    d: (),
};
crate::pr_info!("    original = {:?}", original);

let serialized = kernel::test_serde::to_vec(&original).unwrap();
crate::pr_info!("  serialized = {:?}", serialized);

let deserialized: S = kernel::test_serde::from_vec(&serialized).unwrap();
crate::pr_info!("deserialized = {:?}", deserialized);

Giving:

[    0.788766] rust_serde: Rust serde sample (init)
[    0.788974] rust_serde:     original = S { a: (), b: false, c: true, d: () }
[    0.789443] rust_serde:   serialized = [2, 0, 1, 0, 1, 1, 0, 3]
[    0.789880] rust_serde: deserialized = S { a: (), b: false, c: true, d: () }
[    0.794155] rust_serde: Rust serde sample (exit)
I have to clean it up etc., but I should be able to upload it today for you to play with it if you want it. It is also not complete (e.g. no doc support).
To be clear, the branch is not something we can upstream right away. But it may be useful for experiments and to help you and others make the case for third-party crates.
i.e. similar to the other syn branch we had.

Thank you, I thought you already had one available, I didn't realize you were working on it, I really appreciate it. The example looks really good, deriving Serialize and Deserialize is exactly what I need. Regarding upstreaming, I'm aware that my work will take some time before it could be upstreamed (if it could be upstreamed at all), since I'm missing quite a few dependencies between the third-party crates and the rust filesystem abstractions, but at least I could work on a proof-of-concept and be ready with the kernel module by the time the dependencies are upstreamed

My pleasure! I wasn't working on it, but when I saw your message, I decided to just do it. :)

Uploaded to: https://github.com/Rust-for-Linux/linux/pull/1007

I cleaned things up, added a local (to the kernel module sample) data format test too, to check that they could be defined there too; and a test for serde_derive on the kernel crate. All those tests are not intended to be final at all (we definitely don't want to do things that way in kernel at least), and it was just for me to test, but you may find them useful, so I left it in the branch. I added the rustdoc and rusttest support too.
This looks so much better than what I was trying to accomplish with inlining the entire dependency tree :grinning:
Glad you like it! :)

How can I export rust functions so I can use them from a kernel module? I have a module I can build with y, but not with m because I'm missing some external dependencies and modpost gives me a list of undefined symbols
Can you show us some of those missing symbols?
(deleted)

they are part of an external crate I've integrated called serde_cbor:

<serde_cbor::error::Error as serde::de::Error>::invalid_type
<serde_cbor::read::SliceRead as serde_cbor::read::Read>::offset
<serde_cbor::error::Error>::syntax
<serde_cbor::read::SliceRead>::end
<serde_cbor::read::SliceRead as serde_cbor::read::Read>::clear_buffer
<serde_cbor::read::SliceRead as serde_cbor::read::Read>::read_to_buffer
<serde_cbor::read::SliceRead as serde_cbor::read::Read>::take_buffer
<serde::de::format::Buf>::new
<serde::de::format::Buf>::as_str
<serde::de::format::Buf as core::fmt::Write>::write_str

If you are linking a crate into the kernel with the intent to export it to external modules you will need to add a copy of https://github.com/Rust-for-Linux/linux/blob/bc22545f38d74473cfef3e9fd65432733435b79f/rust/Makefile#L367-L368 for it and the generated file at https://github.com/Rust-for-Linux/linux/blob/bc22545f38d74473cfef3e9fd65432733435b79f/rust/exports.c#L21
thanks :smiley:
